en.extract:
The eastern black-headed batis (Batis minor) is a passerine bird in the family Platysteiridae from eastern Africa. It was formerly treated as conspecific with the western black-headed batis.


Taxonomy
The eastern black-headed batis was described by the German ornithologist Carlo von Erlanger in 1901 who considered the taxon as a subspecies of the grey-headed batis (Batis orientalis) and introduced the trinomial name Batis orientalis minor.
Two subspecies are recognised:

B. m. minor Erlanger, 1901 – southern Somalia
B. m. suahelica Neumann, 1907 – southeastern Kenya and eastern Tanzania


Description
The eastern black-headed batis is 10 cm (3.9 in) in length and weighs 9.3–13.8 g (0.33–0.49 oz). It is a small, stocky, rather restless, flycatcher like bird with a white, black and grey plumage. The forehead, crown and nape are blackish grey with a white supercilium and loral spot, the mask is glossy bluish black and there is a white spot on the nape. The mantle and back are dark grey with a paler rump, females have a greyer mantle. The underparts are white with a glossy black breast band, which is dark reddish brown in the females. The tail is black with white outer tail feathers while the wings are mainly black with a white stripe. The bill and legs are black and the eyes are yellow. Juveniles are similar to females but browner.


Distribution and habitat
The eastern black-headed batis occurs in a wide variety of habitats so long as trees are present. These vary from arid savannah woodlands through to juniper on the edge of montane evergreen forest, and including parks and gardens.


Behaviour
The eastern black-headed batis' biology is little known, they are seemingly typical batises and live in pairs or small family groups. Its diet is insects which it forages for by searching foliage or flycatching.


References

The '''eastern black-headed batis''' ('''''Batis minor''''') is a [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Platysteiridae]] from eastern Africa. It was formerly treated as [[conspecific]] with the [[western black-headed batis]].

==Taxonomy==
The eastern black-headed batis was described by the German ornithologist [[Carlo von Erlanger]] in 1901 who considered the taxon as a subspecies of the [[grey-headed batis]] (''Batis orientalis'') and introduced the [[trinomial name]] ''Batis orientalis minor''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Erlanger | first=Carlo von | author-link=Carlo von Erlanger | year=1901 | title=Einige neue Arten aud Nordostafica | language=de | journal=Ornithologische Monatsberichte | volume=9 | issue=12 | pages=181–183 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32693109 }}</ref>

Two [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2018 | title=Batises, woodshrikes, bushshrikes, vangas | work=World Bird List Version 8.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/batises/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=21 June 2018 }}</ref>
* ''B. m. minor'' Erlanger, 1901 – southern Somalia
* ''B. m. suahelica'' Neumann, 1907 – southeastern Kenya and eastern Tanzania

==Description==
The eastern black-headed batis is {{cvt|10|cm}} in length and weighs {{cvt|9.3|–|13.8|g}}.<ref name=HBW>{{cite web | url = http://www.hbw.com/species/eastern-black-headed-batis-batis-minor | title = Eastern Black-headed Batis (''Batis minor'') | access-date = 2013-11-03 | publisher = Lynx Edicions | work = Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive | url-status = live | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20150822001133/http://www.hbw.com/species/eastern-black-headed-batis-batis-minor | archive-date = 22 August 2015 }}</ref> It is a small, stocky, rather restless, flycatcher like bird with a white, black and grey plumage. The forehead, crown and nape are blackish grey with a white supercilium and loral spot, the mask is glossy bluish black and there is a white spot on the nape. The mantle and back are dark grey with a paler rump, females have a greyer mantle. The underparts are white with a glossy black breast band, which is dark reddish brown in the females. The tail is black with white outer tail feathers while the wings are mainly black with a white stripe. The bill and legs are black and the eyes are yellow. Juveniles are similar to females but browner.<ref name = Shrikes>{{cite book | last1 = Harris | first1 = Tony | last2 = Franklin | first2 = Kim | year = 2000 | title = Shrikes and Bush-shrikes | publisher = Christopher Helm | pages = 313–315 | ISBN = 0-7136-3861-3}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The eastern black-headed batis occurs in a wide variety of habitats so long as trees are present. These vary from arid savannah woodlands through to [[juniper]] on the edge of montane evergreen forest, and including parks and gardens.<ref name = Shrikes/>

==Behaviour==
The eastern black-headed batis' biology is little known, they are seemingly typical [[batis (bird)|batis]]es and live in pairs or small family groups.<ref name = Shrikes/> Its diet is insects which it forages for by searching foliage or flycatching.<ref name = HBW/>
